title: Trinidadian callaloo
subtitle: Dasheen leaves with crab and coconut
excerpt: Dasheen (taro) leaves simmered with whole crab claws, okra, coconut milk, scotch bonnet, and garlic until the leaves have completely broken down and the soup has been beaten to a smooth, dark green purée. Trinidadian callaloo is not a soup in the serving sense. It is a thick, intensely flavoured accompaniment to rice and provision, as central to the Sunday table as the main dish.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian callaloo is one of the national dishes of Trinidad and Tobago, traditionally made with dasheen bush (taro leaves) rather than the amaranth-based callaloo of Jamaica. The crab is a key element, blue crab claws are used in Trinidad, though whole crab pieces work as a substitute. Callaloo appears in Trinidadian culinary records from the 18th century and reflects the West African tradition of leafy-green one-pot dishes brought by enslaved people.

content.text: Callaloo leaves are the right ingredient for this recipe. Spinach is the usual swap if you cannot find dasheen bush. The flavour is quite different though. Dasheen leaves are more mineral and slightly bitter, which is why the coconut milk and crab balance them so well. Spinach callaloo is milder and less complex.

The swizzle step (beating the cooked callaloo vigorously with a wooden stick or hand blender until it forms a smooth, homogeneous purée) is not optional. Unswizzled callaloo has a stringy, chunky texture. After swizzling, it should be completely smooth and very dark green. If using a hand blender, blend for at least 2 full minutes.

items.name: Callaloo leaves
items.prepNote: dasheen bush or spinach, washed, stems removed

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {{onion}}, {{spring-onion}}, and {{garlic}} and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{callaloo-leaves}}, {{okra}}, {{thyme}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole), {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Pour in the {{coconut-milk}} and 200 ml of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a medium simmer. Cover and cook for 30 minutes until the leaves are completely soft and the okra has dissolved. Remove the scotch bonnet. Use a hand blender to beat the callaloo to a completely smooth, dark green purée, this is called swizzling. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve alongside white rice, provision, and the main dish of the Sunday table.

items.fix: Blend for at least 2 full minutes, callaloo leaves are fibrous and take longer to break down than spinach. Keep blending until completely smooth
Dasheen leaves produce a much deeper green. If using spinach, add it in the last 10 minutes rather than at the start to preserve the colour
items.cause: Using spinach rather than dasheen leaves, or overcooked

items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered until it thickens. Properly thick callaloo should hold its shape slightly when spooned onto a plate

content.text: Callaloo is one of the two national dishes of Trinidad and Tobago. The other is crab and callaloo, the stuffed version. It sits on the Sunday table as a needed side. No Sunday lunch is complete without it. The texture and flavour are unlike any other green dish: thick, smooth, rich from the coconut milk, with okra and dasheen leaves layered on top. In Britain the Trinidadian diaspora still makes callaloo as a Sunday staple. They buy dasheen leaves from Caribbean food shops or swap in spinach when they can't find them.
